title: How to Improve Your Credit Score
meta_description: Learn what CIBIL score is, how it's calculated, and 7 practical tips to improve your credit score from 600 to 750+ in India.

hero.one_liner: Your credit score decides if you get loans and credit cards — and at what interest rate. Here's how to build and improve it.

sections.heading: What is a Credit Score?
content: A credit score (also called CIBIL score in India) is a 3-digit number between 300 and 900 that tells lenders how likely you are to repay borrowed money. The higher your score, the more trustworthy you appear to banks. A score of 750 or above is considered good and gets you the best loan and credit card offers.

In India, CIBIL (TransUnion) is the most used credit bureau, but there are three others: Experian, Equifax, and CRIF High Mark. Banks check your score before approving any loan or credit card. A low score means rejection or higher interest rates — which can cost you lakhs over a loan's lifetime.

rows.value: Excellent — best interest rates, instant approvals
Good — most loans approved, competitive rates
Fair — loans possible but at higher interest rates
Poor — difficult to get loans, very high interest
Very Poor — most applications will be rejected

sections.heading: 7 Tips to Improve Your Credit Score

steps.title: Pay credit card bills on time, every time
steps.description: Payment history is the biggest factor (35% of score). Even one missed payment stays on your report for years. Set auto-pay for at least the minimum due amount.

steps.title: Keep credit utilization below 30%
steps.description: If your credit card limit is ₹1 lakh, don't use more than ₹30,000. High utilization signals financial stress. If you regularly hit 80-90%, ask for a limit increase.

steps.title: Don't close old credit cards
steps.description: Length of credit history matters. Your oldest card helps your score even if you rarely use it. Keep it active with a small purchase every few months.

steps.title: Don't apply for too many loans at once
steps.description: Each loan application triggers a 'hard inquiry' that drops your score by 5-10 points. Space out applications by at least 3-6 months.

steps.title: Maintain a mix of credit types
steps.description: Having both secured (home/car loan) and unsecured (credit card/personal loan) credit shows you can handle different types of debt responsibly.

steps.title: Check your CIBIL report for errors
steps.description: Get your free annual credit report from cibil.com. Look for wrong accounts, incorrect payment statuses, or debts you don't recognize. Dispute errors online.

steps.title: Become an authorized user
steps.description: If you have no credit history, ask a family member with good credit to add you as an authorized user on their credit card. Their payment history helps build yours.

faqs.question: How do I check my CIBIL score for free?
faqs.answer: Visit cibil.com and sign up — you get one free report per year. Apps like Paytm, PhonePe, CRED, and Bajaj Finserv also show your score for free with monthly updates.

faqs.question: How long does it take to improve credit score?
faqs.answer: Small improvements (20-50 points) can happen in 1-3 months by paying dues and reducing utilization. Significant improvement (100+ points) typically takes 6-12 months of consistent good behavior.

faqs.question: I have no credit history. What's my score?
faqs.answer: If you've never taken a loan or credit card, you'll have a score of -1 (no history) or very low. Start building credit with a secured credit card or become an authorized user on a family member's card.

faqs.question: Does checking my own score lower it?
faqs.answer: No. Checking your own score is a 'soft inquiry' and has zero impact. Only applications for new loans/cards ('hard inquiries') affect your score.

faqs.question: Will settling a loan hurt my score?
faqs.answer: Yes. 'Settled' status (paying less than full amount) is negative and stays on your report for 7 years. It's better to pay the full amount, even if in installments, rather than settle.

disclaimer: This article is for educational purposes only. Credit scores and their impact vary by lender. Consult a financial advisor for personalized guidance.
